operating_state: First quarter 2022 earnings reached $5.5 billion, up from $2.7 billion in the prior year, driven by 68% higher crude oil realizations and 137% higher natural gas realizations amid supply tightness from pandemic underinvestment and Russia-Ukraine conflict. Oil-equivalent production declined 3% to 3.7 million barrels per day, reflecting reduced Groningen production limits, higher downtime from weather, and lower entitlements from higher prices, partially offset by Permian Basin and Guyana growth.
cost_structure: Upstream margins expanded significantly from higher commodity realizations, while downstream margins improved $310 million driven by refining margin expansion to levels above the 10-year range. Chemical margins declined $20 million despite favorable volume effects. Capital and exploration expenditures increased 57% to $4.9 billion, with upstream spending rising to $3.9 billion from $2.4 billion in the prior year.
material_events: The company announced discontinuation of Sakhalin-1 operations in Russia following the Ukraine invasion, resulting in $3.4 billion in after-tax impairment charges and loss of 150 million oil-equivalent barrels of proved reserves. ExxonMobil initiated share repurchases of $2.1 billion in the quarter and distributed $3.8 billion in dividends. The company announced an organizational restructuring to enhance effectiveness and reduce costs.
macro_exposures: Russia-Ukraine conflict and associated sanctions created material operational disruption, forcing exit from Sakhalin-1 operations with significant asset impairments. Supply chain constraints and commodity market volatility from geopolitical tensions affected global operations. The company faces ongoing exposure to oil and gas price fluctuations amid tight supply-demand balances expected to persist.
forward_outlook: Management plans capital investments of $21-24 billion for 2022, with continued focus on advantaged projects, balance sheet strengthening, and reliable dividend payments. The company expanded its share repurchase program from $10 billion to $30 billion through 2023, though timing depends on market conditions. Sakhalin-1 exit is expected to eliminate hydrocarbon sales and cash flows from the project after the second quarter.
uncertainty_profile: Ongoing U.S. federal tax disputes with the IRS for years beginning 2006 continue through appeals processes, though unfavorable resolution would not materially impact operations. The company maintains various ongoing litigation and contingencies. Actual capital spending and operational results remain subject to project progress, property acquisitions, and market conditions.
